We need to make sure that we are capable of integrating that data so that we can solve complex problems across domains. I guess in in defense, air, space, maritime, land, cyber increasingly that requires that we can connect the dots. and then we need to expose a simplified representation of that data so that a hard-pressed individual operating under pressure can make sense of the data and also so that we can as we increasingly open that data platform to agents so we reduce the risk of hallucination for the agents as well. So those are three principles that I think have stood the test of time. this word data modernization a lot right now in the industry a a difficult term I think I I I would say that data modernization can mean whatever the vendor sat across you wants to sell you that day. The first is this idea of open table formats and and the the promise of open table formats is that we can store more data more reliably and crucially more cost-ffectively and cost effective is important. I was speaking to the ministry of defense at one of the smaller European nations last week their next generation AI data platform that they envisaged to integrate those five domains they think will be a minimum of 2.5 pabytes of data. for a country of the size complexity of India I think you can multiply that by at least 10. So being able to store large volumes of of data and to to fuse that data that sensor data cost effectively is very important. But the more important consequence of open table formats is this idea of interoperability. This idea that we cannot possibly predict sat here in this room today which models and which technologies will be the most important four or five years from now. And this idea that different technologies, different processing engines have different strengths and weaknesses for different applications. And so we need to be able to support many of them. And so the idea of having a data foundation that we can bring multiple tools to is incredibly important in that conversation. Well, we're building this data foundation so that we can bring AI to that data foundation and increasingly so we can bring agentic AI to that foundation. And I think there are a couple of things that are important to to think through there. The first is I think we can get very wrapped up around the idea of training models around training the best model around training our own model. I think that's a little bit of a trap. Honestly, when I look at the performance of the open- source models, they're generally only six months behind the proprietary models. So, right now, the open source models are catching up with the proprietary models inside six months. And those open source models, we can inspect the architecture, we can inspect the weights. So, we can we can be pretty confident in those models. And so, I think we should focus less on training our own models and think more about inference. confident in those models. And so, I think we should focus less on training our own models and think more about inference. So better, cheaper, faster and more reliable. That's a pretty good trade, right? So we should think about not having one single model to rule them all. We need to start thinking about the models that are available to us as a box of tools. And when we have a box of tools, we shouldn't always reach for the hammer just because it's the biggest shiniest tool in the box. And the great promise of AI that we're seeing play out already is that we can now apply these models to unstructured data to images to audio to text and we can have multimodal intelligence and this is incredibly important.
